epigraph.text: Now to him who is able to do far more abundantly than all that we ask or think.
epigraph.quote: The moving power of mathematical invention is not reasoning but imagination.

content.text:
We now define what it means for a function to be differentiable in the 
complex sense. This definition looks identical to the real case, but the 
two-dimensional approach creates profound consequences.

content.title: Complex Derivative
content.content:
Let $f$ be defined on a neighborhood of $z_0$. The 
**derivative** of $f$ at $z_0$ is
\[
 f'(z_0) = \lim_{h \to 0} \frac{f(z_0 + h) - f(z_0)}{h},
\]
provided this limit exists. If $f'(z_0)$ exists, we say $f$ is 
**differentiable** at $z_0$.

content.text:
The key point: $h$ is a complex number, so it can approach $0$ from any 
direction. For the limit to exist, the difference quotient must converge 
to the same value along *every* path.

content.title: Analytic and Holomorphic Functions
content.content:
A function $f$ is **analytic** (or 
**holomorphic**) on an open set $D$ if 
$f'(z)$ exists for every $z \in D$. A function analytic on all of $\C$ 
is called **entire**.

content.text: The familiar rules from real calculus extend unchanged.

content.title: Differentiation Rules
content.content:
If $f$ and $g$ are differentiable at $z$, then:

1. $(f + g)'(z) = f'(z) + g'(z)$

2. $(cf)'(z) = cf'(z)$ for any constant $c$

3. $(fg)'(z) = f'(z)g(z) + f(z)g'(z)$ (product rule)

4. $(f/g)'(z) = \dfrac{f'(z)g(z) - f(z)g'(z)}{[g(z)]^2}$ 
 (quotient rule, $g(z) \neq 0$)

5. $(g \circ f)'(z) = g'(f(z)) \cdot f'(z)$ (chain rule)
content.label: Theorem

content.text: The proofs are identical to the real case and are left as exercises.

content.title: Derivatives of Powers
content.content:
For any positive integer $n$,
\[
 \frac{d}{dz}(z^n) = nz^{n-1}.
\]
More generally, if $f(z) = az^n + bz^{n-1} + \cdots + c$ is a polynomial, 
then $f'(z) = naz^{n-1} + (n-1)bz^{n-2} + \cdots$
content.label: Theorem

content.content:
We use the binomial theorem:
$$
 \frac{(z + h)^n - z^n}{h} &= \frac{\sum_{k=0}^{n} \binom{n}{k}z^{n-k}h^k - z^n}{h} \\
 &= \sum_{k=1}^{n} \binom{n}{k}z^{n-k}h^{k-1} = nz^{n-1} + \binom{n}{2}z^{n-2}h + \cdots
$$
As $h \to 0$, all terms but the first vanish.

content.title: The conjugate function is not differentiable
content.solution:
Compute the difference quotient:
\[
 \frac{f(z + h) - f(z)}{h} = \frac{\conj{z + h} - \conj{z}}{h} 
 = \frac{\conj{h}}{h}.
\]

If $h = t$ is real and positive, $\conj{h}/h = t/t = 1$.

If $h = it$ with $t > 0$ real, $\conj{h}/h = -it/(it) = -1$.

The limit depends on direction, so $f'(z)$ does not exist for any $z$.

content.text:
This example is fundamental. The function $f(z) = \conj{z}$—perfectly 
continuous, smooth in the real sense—fails complex differentiability 
everywhere. The complex derivative is a stringent requirement.

marginNotes.text:
There is one glory of the sun, and 
another glory of the moon, and another glory of the stars; for star differs 
from star in glory.

marginNotes.author: Bernhard Riemann
marginNotes.text:
Complex differentiation is more than 
real differentiation applied twice.
